text:
The implementation is complete. I could not execute the Node test runner — every `node` invocation in this environment returns "This command requires approval," which isn't being granted, so I'm surfacing that rather than retrying further. I verified the logic by hand-tracing it against the full semver.org precedence chain instead.

## What changed

`semver.js` previously split on `-` and threw away everything after it, so it only compared `major.minor.patch` and treated `1.0.0-alpha` as equal to `1.0.0`. I replaced it with a general comparator that implements the SemVer §11 precedence rules:

1. **Core**: compare `major`, `minor`, `patch` numerically.
2. **Presence of pre-release**: if cores are equal, a version *with* a pre-release tag is lower than one *without* (`1.0.0-alpha` < `1.0.0`).
3. **Identifier-by-identifier**, left to right: both-numeric compared numerically (`alpha.2` < `alpha.10`); a numeric identifier ranks below an alphanumeric one (`1` < `alpha`); both-alphanumeric compared by ASCII order.
4. **Tie-break**: when all leading identifiers are equal, fewer identifiers is lower (`alpha.1` < `alpha.1.1`).

It's driven entirely by the parsed structure — `/^\d+$/` to classify an identifier as numeric, generic loops over the split arrays — with no per-input branches or lookup tables, so it generalizes to any version string of the form `X.Y.Z[-pre.identifiers]`.

## Verification

I hand-traced the canonical ordered chain `1.0.0-alpha < alpha.1 < alpha.beta < beta < beta.2 < beta.11 < rc.1 < 1.0.0` plus the numeric-vs-alphanumeric and fewer-identifiers cases — all produce the expected `-1`, and each is antisymmetric (`cmp(b,a) === -cmp(a,b)`). I have *not* been able to confirm this by actually running the code.

I left a ready-to-run test at `/private/tmp/big-RuU7bn/test_semver_check.js` — if you run `node test_semver_check.js` (or approve the `node` permission), it will report passed/failed counts and exit non-zero on any failure. Want me to remove that test file, or were the `node` approvals something you can enable so I can run it myself?
